items.title: Trump signs executive order on post-quantum cryptography
items.summary: President Trump signed an executive order directing federal agencies to accelerate migration to post-quantum cryptography to protect sensitive data.
items.details: The June 22 order directs the Office of Management and Budget and the National Cyber Director to lead a nationwide migration to post-quantum cryptography. It also tasks the Department of Commerce, NSA, and DHS with issuing guidance to agencies on the transition.

items.title: DOJ investigation of California Governor Newsom reported
items.summary: Governor Gavin Newsom said he and his wife are under investigation by the U.S. Department of Justice; the department has not confirmed a probe.
items.details: In a video posted on X, Newsom said the investigation was politically motivated and tied to his consideration of a 2028 presidential run. He said investigators were also focusing on his wife, Jennifer Siebel Newsom. The Department of Justice has not confirmed any investigation.

items.title: U.S. and Iran sign interim agreement to end war
items.summary: President Trump and Iranian President Pezeshkian signed a memorandum of understanding outlining terms to end the war and reopen the Strait of Hormuz.
items.details: The interim agreement, signed June 17, starts a 60-day negotiating window on Iran's nuclear program. Under the 14-point memorandum, the U.S. is expected to lift sanctions and unfreeze assets, while Iran agreed to reopen the Strait of Hormuz and reaffirmed it will not develop nuclear weapons.

items.title: China sanctions U.S. military-related companies
items.summary: China announced sanctions on 10 American military-related companies in response to a U.S. move barring some Chinese tech firms from defense contracts.
items.details: China announced the sanctions on June 22 after the U.S. moved to bar some leading Chinese technology companies from defense contracts.

items.title: Congress passes housing affordability bill
items.summary: The House passed the 21st Century Road to Housing Act 358-32, following the Senate's 85-5 vote, sending the bipartisan measure toward becoming law.
items.details: The bill aims to increase housing supply by easing construction regulations and restricting large investors from buying single-family homes. The 32 House no votes were all cast by Republicans, while all voting Democrats supported it.

items.title: Trump cancels housing bill signing ceremony
items.summary: President Trump canceled a planned signing ceremony for the housing bill, saying Congress must first pass the SAVE America Act voter-identification bill.
items.details: Trump announced on social media that the signing was canceled until Congress passes the SAVE America Act, a voter ID measure. The bill can still become law without his signature, and he has not indicated he intends to veto it.

items.title: Supreme Court rules on Temporary Protected Status
items.summary: In a 6-3 decision in Mullin v. Doe, the Supreme Court ruled the president has unreviewable authority to end the Temporary Protected Status program.
items.details: Writing for the majority, Justice Samuel Alito held that under the TPS law the president may end the program without court intervention. The ruling allows the administration to proceed with removing people who had been living and working legally under TPS.

items.title: Supreme Court rules on green card holders' reentry
items.summary: In a 6-3 opinion, the Supreme Court ruled federal border officials can use an indictment to temporarily strip green cards from immigrants reentering the country.
items.details: Justice Clarence Thomas wrote that officials need not have clear and convincing evidence of offenses to suspend a lawful permanent resident's status. Justice Ketanji Brown Jackson, joined by Justices Kagan and Sotomayor, dissented.

items.title: Supreme Court rules on firearms and private property
items.summary: In a 6-3 decision in Wolford v. Lopez, the Supreme Court ruled states cannot require gun owners to get permission from property owners before bringing firearms onto their land.
items.details: The court said requiring advance permission is an undue burden on the right to possess and carry a firearm. Five states had passed laws requiring such permission in advance.

items.title: Mamdani-backed candidates win New York primaries
items.summary: Three progressive candidates backed by New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani won their primary elections in safe seats, including two who challenged Democratic incumbents.
items.details: All three candidates won their primaries on June 23 in safe seats, making their November election nearly certain. The results were part of a slate of contested House primaries.

items.title: Supreme Court clears Alabama congressional map
items.summary: The Supreme Court cleared the way for Alabama to use a congressional map originally drawn in 2023 for the 2026 midterms.
items.details: A federal court had previously barred the map from being implemented after finding it discriminated against Black voters. The map is likely to reduce the number of Democratic-held House seats in the state from two to one.

items.title: Federal appeals court allows expanded expedited deportations
items.summary: A federal appeals court allowed the administration to resume carrying out speedy deportations of undocumented migrants throughout the U.S., not just near the border.
items.details: The June 24 ruling permits expedited removals to be conducted nationwide rather than limited to areas near the border.

GA.title: Georgia lawmakers decline to redraw maps in special session
GA.summary: Georgia's Republican legislative leaders declined to take up redistricting during a special session called by Governor Brian Kemp.
GA.details: House Speaker Jon Burns informed Kemp in a letter that legislators would not consider congressional or legislative redistricting for the 2028 cycle during the session, citing pending litigation. Kemp had called the session after the Supreme Court's Louisiana v. Callais ruling.

CA.title: California launches AI job-loss tracking tool
CA.summary: California introduced what it described as a first-in-the-nation online tool to monitor and detect AI-related job loss, part of Governor Newsom's executive order on AI and the workforce.
CA.details: The tool was announced June 25 as part of the governor's executive order on artificial intelligence and the workforce. It is intended to track and detect job losses related to AI.
CA.source: Office of the Governor of California

NY.title: New York holds primary elections
NY.summary: New York held primary elections on June 23 for races including governor and U.S. House seats.
NY.details: Among the results, progressive candidates backed by New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani won several House primaries, including challenges to Democratic incumbents. Bruce Blakeman ran in the Republican primary for governor.

CT.title: Connecticut enacts youth online safety and AI law
CT.summary: Governor Ned Lamont signed legislation establishing youth online safety protections, regulations on artificial intelligence, and workforce training initiatives.
CT.details: Public Act 26-15 was developed by Governor Lamont, Attorney General William Tong, and State Senator James Maroney. The bill addresses youth social media use, AI decision-making transparency, and workforce training for tech jobs.

NH.title: New Hampshire governor signs and vetoes bills
NH.summary: Governor Kelly Ayotte signed 18 bills into law and issued vetoes on several measures.
NH.details: The signed bills addressed topics including school district federal grants, registry filing deadlines, and vehicle registration documentation. Ayotte vetoed measures including HB 1072 on wage enforcement authority and SB 468 on marijuana cultivation.

VT.title: Vermont governor signs slate of bills
VT.summary: Governor Phil Scott signed several bills into law on June 8 covering criminal justice, health, animal welfare, and education.
VT.details: The signed bills included measures on officer-involved shooting protocols, gender equity in correctional facilities, penalties for animal cruelty offenses, and addressing chronic absenteeism in schools.

IA.title: Iowa governor signs final bills of 2026 session
IA.summary: Governor Kim Reynolds signed the final bills from the 2026 legislative session, including measures on gubernatorial powers and early childhood programs.
IA.details: The June 2 signings were the last of the session and Reynolds's final as governor, as she is not seeking re-election in 2026. The bills included one restricting certain gubernatorial powers.

disclaimer: This digest reports verifiable developments from cited news sources and does not endorse any party, candidate, or position.
